skill.name: typescript-jsdoc
skill.description: Write effective JSDoc comments for TypeScript code, with guidance on format, placement, examples, and avoiding redundant documentation.
skill.summary: Guide TypeScript teams to write useful JSDoc that explains behavior, intent, and API contracts.
skill.author: AdamManuel-dev

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, crypto, network, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found them inside Markdown guidance and TypeScript documentation examples. No executable scripts, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious intent were found; the skill is safe to publish with low residual risk from example text only.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Examples Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The external command detections point to Markdown code fences, inline JSDoc syntax, and TypeScript examples rather than executable shell commands. These files provide documentation guidance and are not runnable scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is clearly inside Markdown prose or fenced TypeScript examples. No file contains executable Ruby, shell, or script logic that would run commands.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Documentation Fetch Example Flagged as Network Use
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The network finding is a TypeScript example showing a function named fetch in a JSDoc reference section. It does not call an external endpoint or transmit data from the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only network-related match is documentation text inside a fenced TypeScript example. There is no runtime implementation or outbound URL in the skill files.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Security Terms Flagged as Sensitive Access
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Terms such as certificatePath, host, port, and SSL appear as example parameter names for teaching nested JSDoc comments. They do not access local files, certificates, credentials, or system information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged terms are part of explanatory documentation examples. I found no evidence of file reads, credential handling, cryptographic operations, or reconnaissance commands.

content.value_statement: TypeScript teams often write comments that repeat types or miss important behavior. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to create JSDoc that explains intent, contracts, examples, and edge cases.
actual_capabilities: Explains when TypeScript code benefits from JSDoc and when comments are redundant.
Provides JSDoc formats for functions, classes, services, React components, and generic functions.
Guides documentation for parameters, return values, thrown errors, examples, deprecations, and related APIs.
Shows how to document complex object parameters and nested configuration fields.
Identifies anti-patterns such as repeating type information or documenting unstable implementation details.
limitations: Does not generate documentation from a full repository automatically without code context.
Does not validate JSDoc output against a TypeScript compiler or documentation generator.
Does not define a project-specific documentation style beyond the guidance in the skill.
May need user guidance for private domain terms, business rules, and API lifecycle policies.

use_cases.title: Document Public Library APIs
use_cases.description: Create useful JSDoc for exported TypeScript functions, classes, and interfaces before release.

use_cases.title: Improve Team Code Reviews
use_cases.description: Check whether comments explain behavior, side effects, errors, and examples instead of repeating types.

use_cases.title: Clarify Complex Application Code
use_cases.description: Add concise documentation for async flows, generic utilities, configuration objects, and non-obvious behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Add Basic JSDoc
prompt_templates.prompt: Write concise JSDoc for this TypeScript function. Focus on purpose, parameters, return value, and any important edge cases.

prompt_templates.title: Review Existing Comments
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these JSDoc comments for a TypeScript file. Identify redundant comments, missing behavior notes, unclear examples, and outdated implementation details.

prompt_templates.title: Document a Public API
prompt_templates.scenario: Prepare exported code for other developers
prompt_templates.prompt: Create JSDoc for these exported TypeScript APIs. Include usage guidance, thrown errors, examples, and deprecation notes where appropriate.

prompt_templates.title: Set Documentation Standards
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a TypeScript JSDoc review checklist for this project. Cover public APIs, async behavior, generics, complex parameters, examples, and anti-patterns.

output_examples.input: A TypeScript payment function with retry behavior and several possible errors.
output: A concise summary of what the function does.
Parameter descriptions focused on business meaning.
Notes about retry timing, failure modes, and expected caller behavior.
A short usage example that shows success and error handling.

output_examples.input: An exported React component with editable and read-only modes.
output: Component-level documentation that explains its role in the UI.
Prop descriptions that clarify defaults and callback responsibilities.
A usage example for the common read-only case and the editable case.

output_examples.input: A generic utility function with caching and an optional key extractor.
output: Template parameter explanations that describe how the types relate.
Behavior notes for cache invalidation and identity comparison.
Guidance on when callers should provide a custom key extractor.

best_practices: Use JSDoc for public APIs, complex behavior, side effects, errors, and examples.
Explain intent and contracts instead of repeating TypeScript type annotations.
Update comments whenever behavior, errors, performance, or API lifecycle changes.
anti_patterns: Do not document every obvious private helper just to satisfy a comment quota.
Do not describe implementation details that may change while the contract stays stable.
Do not write parameter descriptions that only restate primitive TypeScript types.

faq.question: Does this skill replace TypeScript types?
faq.answer: No. It complements types by explaining purpose, behavior, examples, errors, and decisions.

faq.question: When should I add JSDoc to TypeScript code?
faq.answer: Add it to public APIs, shared modules, complex logic, async behavior, and code with important side effects.

faq.question: Can it help with React components?
faq.answer: Yes. The reference includes guidance for documenting component props, defaults, callbacks, and usage examples.

faq.question: Will it generate documentation sites?
faq.answer: No. It helps write source comments, but it does not run documentation generators or publish sites.

faq.question: Can it review existing comments?
faq.answer: Yes. It can identify redundant, outdated, vague, or implementation-dependent comments when you provide the code.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to use on private code?
faq.answer: The skill contains documentation guidance only. Review your AI tool settings before sharing private source code.
